title: Hormuz Blockade Returns as US Strikes Iran, Kirkuk Pipeline Gets US Backing, Özel Prepares New Party
subhead: The US reimposed a naval blockade on Iran and launched strikes on Iranian coastal targets, renewing pressure on Turkey's energy and trade routes. Meanwhile, Washington endorsed a Kirkuk-Baniyas oil pipeline through Syria, and opposition leader Özgür Özel signaled he may form a new party if legal appeals fail.

sections.heading: US Reimposes Hormuz Blockade, Strikes Iranian Targets
sections.body:
The United States launched two waves of strikes on Iranian coastal defenses and missile sites on July 15 and reimposed a naval blockade of Iranian ports, according to Reuters. Iran retaliated by targeting US military sites in neighboring countries, calling the conflict an 'existential war.' President Trump backed off an initial plan to impose a 20 percent transit toll through the Strait of Hormuz but affirmed the blockade. Iranian officials reported that US strikes have killed more than 35 people and wounded over 300.

Shipping companies are already refusing US-military-guided transits through Hormuz after recent Iranian attacks on commercial vessels, according to Reuters. The renewed closure of the world's most critical oil chokepoint directly impacts Turkey, which depends on Gulf oil shipments and has been positioning its Middle Corridor as a Hormuz bypass route. Oil prices fell 5% to a three-month low on hopes of a reopening, but the blockade's reimposition threatens to reverse that trend.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's energy costs and trade-route positioning are directly exposed to every Hormuz escalation. The blockade's return undermines the interim US-Iran deal announced at the G7 and reinforces the strategic value of Turkey's overland corridor alternatives.

sections.heading: US Backs Kirkuk-Baniysa Pipeline Through Syria
sections.body:
A US State Department official confirmed on July 14 that Washington is supporting efforts by Iraq and Syria to revive the Kirkuk-Baniyas crude pipeline as part of a broader strategy to reduce dependence on the Strait of Hormuz. Bloomberg reported that Chevron could play a role in rebuilding the pipeline, which would carry Iraqi crude from Kirkuk to the Syrian Mediterranean port of Baniyas.

The pipeline, originally built in the 1950s and deactivated since 2003, would run through territory now controlled by the Syrian government of Ahmad Al Shara — Turkey's primary regional ally. The project would directly compete with Turkey's own Ceyhan oil terminal for Iraq's northern export volumes, but could also position Turkey as a transit route for the pipeline's northern extension.
sections.whyItMatters: A revived Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline reroutes Iraqi crude away from Hormuz and through Syria's new government — Turkey's closest partner in the region. This creates both competition for Ceyhan and opportunity for Ankara to shape the corridor's terms.

sections.heading: Iraq's US Troop Withdrawal Redraws Regional Security Map
sections.body:
President Trump and Iraqi Prime Minister Ali Falah al-Zaidi jointly announced on July 15 that all remaining US forces — believed to number fewer than 2,000 — will leave Iraq by September 30, 2026, ending a 23-year military presence. Al-Zaidi framed the shift succinctly: 'US forces will be out of Iraq. US companies will be inside Iraq.'

The withdrawal is conditional on Iraq disarming Iran-backed militias by the same deadline. According to Al Hurra, al-Zaidi presented Trump with a 'clear roadmap on weapons consolidation, with a final, irreversible timeline' of September 30. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Washington expects Iraqi Security Forces and Kurdish Peshmerga to lead counter-ISIS efforts.

For Turkey, the departure removes a US military presence on its southern border and creates a power vacuum that could intensify PKK operations in northern Iraq — or, alternatively, enable closer Ankara-Baghdad security coordination if the new government can consolidate control.
sections.whyItMatters: The US departure from Iraq creates a security vacuum on Turkey's doorstep. Whether that vacuum is filled by a functional Iraqi state or by Iran-backed militias will directly determine PKK operational freedom and Turkey's border security calculus.

sections.heading: Özel Signals New Party as CHP Legal Crisis Deepens
sections.body:
Özgür Özel, the ousted leader of Turkey's main opposition CHP, said on July 15 that preparations are under way for a possible new political party, with a formal step possible 'towards the end of July or the beginning of August,' according to Reuters. Özel was speaking in an interview with Sözcü TV.

His remarks come after a Turkish court annulled the CHP's 2023 congress that elected Özel as chairman, citing irregularities, and reinstated former leader Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu. The court ruling, widely described as politically motivated, has deepened the CHP's internal crisis. Özel said any party formation would wait until after ongoing legal proceedings — an appeal and a request for an extraordinary congress — are resolved, which he estimated could happen within a couple of weeks.
sections.whyItMatters: If Özel follows through, Turkey's opposition landscape could split between a CHP still led by Kılıçdaroğlu (by court order) and a new party built around Özel and the current CHP base. The fragmentation could benefit Erdoğan ahead of elections due by 2028 — or, if a new party consolidates the anti-government vote, create a more dynamic opposition.

sections.heading: S-400 to UAE? Israel Blocks F-35 Path as Turkey Explores Sale
sections.body:
Turkey is exploring the sale of its Russian S-400 air defense systems to the UAE, according to Forbes and regional media reports on July 13-15. The potential transfer is part of Ankara's effort to clear the way for reentry into the US F-35 program, from which it was expelled in 2019 for acquiring the Russian systems.

However, Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu has publicly opposed any F-35 sale to Turkey, telling CNN on July 7 that 'it would destroy the power balance in the Middle East' because 'Turkey has aggressive aspirations.' Forbes reports this is the first time Israel has opposed a US arms sale to a NATO ally — though Israel has a long history of opposing advanced fighter sales to regional states, including Saudi Arabia's F-15 acquisitions in 1978 and 1992.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's S-400-to-UAE gambit is its clearest path back to the F-35, but Israel's opposition introduces a powerful lobbying obstacle in Washington. The outcome will determine whether Turkey's next-generation fighter fleet is American-built or relies on the domestically produced Kaan.

sections.heading: Turkey's Hydro Surge Slashes Gas Use, Eases Energy Bill
sections.body:
According to a Reuters analysis published July 15, Turkey's power producers have slashed natural gas consumption by replacing it with surging hydroelectric output during January to May 2026. Abundant rainfall filled reservoirs across Anatolia, allowing utilities to reduce gas-fired generation at a time when global gas prices remain elevated due to the Iran war.

The timing is significant: as the Hormuz disruption pushes up energy costs globally, Turkey's hydro surplus provides a partial buffer, reducing import dependence and easing pressure on the current account. The question is whether the hydro advantage is structural or weather-dependent — and thus temporary.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's hydroelectric windfall is a rare piece of positive economic news amid the Hormuz crisis. It reduces gas import costs and, if sustained, could help the central bank's disinflation trajectory — but dry years would erase the advantage quickly.

sections.heading: Syria Reshuffles Intelligence, Turkey's Ally Consolidates Control
sections.body:
Syrian President Ahmad Al Shara has appointed Abdulqader Al Tahhan as chief of intelligence, replacing Hussein Al Salama, who moves to head the National Security Council, according to The National on July 15. A Western diplomat described Tahhan as 'one of the most sophisticated security figures in the new system,' noting his flexibility and willingness to make hard decisions.

The reshuffle appears aimed at resolving turf warfare between the Interior Ministry and intelligence services — both run by former Nusra Front co-founders. Tahhan, who led HTS operations in Aleppo during the 2024 offensive that ousted Assad, has been handling the sensitive Druze-majority Sweida province. The National reports that Turkey, Al Shara's main regional backer, has supported the consolidation.
sections.whyItMatters: A more coherent Syrian intelligence apparatus under Turkish-backed leadership could improve coordination on border security and counter-terrorism with Ankara — but the Nusra Front origins of the new intelligence chief will reinforce Western skepticism about the Shara government.

bottomLine: Turkey woke up to a dramatically different regional security landscape: the US-Iran conflict escalated back into open military confrontation with a Hormuz blockade, while Washington endorsed a Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline that would reroute Iraqi oil through Syria. The full US military withdrawal from Iraq by September 30 removes a stabilizing force from Turkey's southern border. At home, Özgür Özel's new party signal adds another dimension to the opposition crisis, and the S-400-to-UAE gambit faces a new Israeli obstacle. Turkey's hydroelectric windfall offers a thin silver lining on the energy front, but the strategic environment is shifting fast — and mostly in directions that demand Ankara's attention.
sources: Reuters, Forbes, The National, Al Hurra, Kashmir Reader, Outlook India, AP, USNews/Reuters, WSWS, Yahoo News/Reuters, Daily Sabah, ECFR, NYT, Fox News, Bloomberg
